questions.question: Define tree traversal and explain why it is different from linear data structure traversal.
keyPoints: Tree traversal is visiting each node exactly once in a systematic manner
Trees are hierarchical, not linear like arrays or linked lists
Linear structures have single path; trees have multiple paths
Requires special strategies: inorder, preorder, postorder, level order

questions.question: Explain Inorder traversal with its algorithm and write one application.
keyPoints: Order: Left → Root → Right (LNR)
Algorithm: Recursively traverse left, visit root, recursively traverse right
Application: Produces sorted output for Binary Search Tree
Can be implemented iteratively using a stack

questions.question: Write the algorithm for Level Order traversal using a queue.
keyPoints: Initialize queue with root node
While queue is not empty: dequeue node, visit it, enqueue left child if exists, enqueue right child if exists
Breadth-first approach
Time complexity O(n), space complexity O(w) where w is maximum width

questions.question: Differentiate between Depth-First Search (DFS) and Breadth-First Search (BFS) in tree traversals.
keyPoints: DFS: explores deep before wide, uses stack or recursion, includes preorder/inorder/postorder
BFS (Level Order): explores level by level, uses queue
DFS: O(h) space where h is height; BFS: O(w) space where w is width
DFS suitable for path finding; BFS suitable for shortest path in unweighted tree

questions.question: What are threaded binary trees? How do they help in traversal?
keyPoints: Threaded binary trees replace NULL pointers with threads to inorder predecessor/successor
Right threads point to inorder successor, left threads to predecessor
Enables traversal without stack or recursion
Saves O(h) auxiliary space during traversal

questions.question: Explain Morris Inorder traversal and its advantage.
keyPoints: Morris traversal achieves O(1) space complexity
Uses threaded links to find inorder predecessor temporarily
Creates links while going left, removes links while returning
No recursion stack or explicit stack required

questions.question: For a binary tree with root R, left child A, and right child B (where A has left child C), show the sequence of nodes visited in all four traversal methods.
keyPoints: Preorder (NLR): R A C B - Root first, then left subtree, then right
Inorder (LNR): C A R B - Left subtree, root, right subtree
Postorder (LRN): C A B R - Children first, root last
Level Order: R A B C - Level by level, left to right

questions.question: Write recursive and iterative algorithms for Inorder traversal of a binary tree. Analyze their time and space complexities.
keyPoints: Recursive: traverse left, visit root, traverse right - O(n) time, O(h) space
Iterative: use stack, push left children, pop and visit, move to right - O(n) time, O(h) space
Space is O(h) where h is height; worst case (skewed) is O(n), best case (balanced) is O(log n)
Iterative avoids recursion stack overflow for deep trees

questions.question: Explain Preorder, Inorder, and Postorder traversals with examples. Discuss one practical application of each.
keyPoints: Preorder (NLR): Root→Left→Right - Application: copying/serializing a tree, prefix expression
Inorder (LNR): Left→Root→Right - Application: BST sorted output, infix expression
Postorder (LRN): Left→Right→Root - Application: deleting tree, postfix expression evaluation
Example tree with 5 nodes should show different output sequences for each

questions.question: A binary tree has nodes with values: Root=10, Left=5, Right=15, Left-Left=2, Left-Right=7. Perform all traversals and show the output sequence.
keyPoints: Tree structure: 10 is root, 5 is left child, 15 is right child, 2 is left of 5, 7 is right of 5
Preorder: 10 5 2 7 15 - Root first
Inorder: 2 5 7 10 15 - Sorted order
Postorder: 2 7 5 15 10 - Children before root
Level Order: 10 5 15 2 7 - Level by level

questions.question: Compare and contrast recursive and iterative tree traversals. Under what circumstances would you choose one over the other?
keyPoints: Recursive: simpler code, uses call stack, risk of stack overflow for deep trees
Iterative: more complex, uses explicit data structure, safer for large trees
Recursive space: O(h), Iterative space: O(h) for stack-based, O(1) for Morris
Choose recursive for small-medium trees, iterative for memory-constrained or very deep trees
